speaker: interviewer
text: --1996, and we're speaking with Sol Einhorn. Sol, we went through three concentration camps already. And I think we're on the way to the fourth. Well, and the third one is where you were taught a trade, right? That's what you were talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Funfteichen. Then after Funfteichen-- Funfteichen was a-- was a labor camp. A lot of people. I would say, a nice few thousand people. And mostly Jewish people, I would say. And the clock factories. It was close to a clock factories. And they had, in the mountains, in the tunnels, they were working at the-- at the-- at that place. Funfteichen, I spend about two or three months, till in January, 1945, in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you, how were you able to keep track of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't keep track of time at that time. But I look back on it today. I visual-- I know what the timing was and where, when we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have--

speaker: interviewee
text: As I-- I would give or take a month or so or two weeks. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the camps, did you have any feeling of when it was the Shabbos or yontif, or any special days?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one day was like the rest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we was-- were all-- it all was gone, all lost. All lost. Whoa I had no-- no-- no calender, no papers, no nothing to guide ourselves, or to know what the-- some days I didn't even know if it's Monday or Tuesday or the Saturday. Or the-- well, the-- the mind was so-- so closed in, the only thing what you're taught is how to survive. And how to take something in the mouth, or how to find something on the floor, a-- a-- a potato, or the piece of food, or then anything. Walking around, just to survive. So we didn't think of anything else. But-- but the-- the only thing, the thought was we hope that tomorrow will be a better day. And that meant by-- and came the evening, and I put my head down. I only pray to God, tomorrow I'll survive. I'm going to keep on going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you were in the camps, when you were in the bunks when you weren't working, were you allowed to talk to the other people or were the guards very strict? Did you have to be quiet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, were. Not in the bunk when we went to sleep, but outside. We had some period of time where we'd talk to each other, and communicated. And maybe a few minutes and quietly, in the barrack next to where the guy slept upstairs or downstairs, or something like that. We communicated very, very little, little, little, as possible--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: --when they kept us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any communication with any of the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get to know any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No one whatsoever. I just saw. Looked at them. They looked at us. Never heard any-- any words, and any-- any conversation with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the camps anywhere near towns, where you could see people-- townspeople coming by? Or did they look in the camp? Or were they aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The only time is when we marched from one camp to the other. We marched through those towns. And people were dying on the roads. Mhm. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And townspeople could see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. Sure. They were nothing. Hiding. The way we walked, the way we were dressed, the way it was winter. It was freezing, but-- and we had no-- no clothes. No--

speaker: interviewer
text: No overcoat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No overcoat. And we had just something, a cover, some schmata over the-- over the hat, all over the shoulders, and freezing, below zero. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so then you were taken to the other camp. What-- do you know what reasons, you were moved around from one camp to the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we-- we were talking within ourselves that the front is-- the front is coming closer. Some rumors went around that we are moving because, you know, the-- the front, the-- the-- the-- the fighting is coming closer into Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that with each camp? Was that the same reason, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, Especially the time when Funfteichen to-- from Dyhernfurth to Funfteichen, no. We didn't know what reason. Then at Funfteichen they start talking rumors there. They-- And we believed, didn't believe. Still, the talk didn't mean that-- that the-- the main thing was survival. That that talk was how to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the day, it was how to survive the night. How to survive, that was the main aim. Not political, or now, nothing, anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said that, you know, your main thing was try to get food, a potato, or a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get them from? Were you given-- like, weren't you given like, regular meals or rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got, then, no regular--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not regular meals, but something to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got-- no, whatever we got, we got once a day. The soup and the piece of-- and the bread. And in the morning we had coffee. Though still, there was some-- a few other. At work you could find a-- a piece of-- of-- skin from potatoes, or a piece of potato, the frozen potato outside. Oh, then in the camp, you go to the near the kitchens. If you walked around, you could have find some-- something on the floor, so just to put in the mouth. That's-- that's-- you scour, walking like dogs look for something to eat, or something like this. We were not-- the mind didn't work as human and you know what I mean. They-- they made us-- the mind was-- the different way of thinking. I mean, only survival, the moldy food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the prisoners good to each other? Or did they fight each other for a scrap of bread or a piece of potato peel?

speaker: interviewee
text: They what-- they didn't have what to fight. I mean, there wasn't what to fight about. There wasn't. There wasn't any-- anything to fight about. The only thing is the leadership from the prisoners, the couples, who they elected, well-- well, mostly-- in most of the camp were Germans. And they were Polish. Or they were the political prisoners, or whatever, from the jails. They had sticks, and they were beating-- they were-- they were-- they were punishing the-- the prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do the prisoners try to help each other? Or was it every man for himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime. Sometime. Sometime.

speaker: interviewer
text: In any of the camps, did you meet people that you knew from other-- you know, other towns, or even from your own town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Met? No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your brother all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers, one of them I left in Auschwitz, older. And the one-- the other one escaped. So and then they brought him into the camps. But different camps. I don't know where, what the names, where, what camps, of this date. And I know one of them got shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so now you were through with the third camp. What was the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, Funfteichen. Funfteichen, there I had a-- still, I had a friend of mine from my hometown. What we came from Auschwitz, Dyhernfurth, and Funfteichen. His name was Yankev Hirsch. Yankev Hirsch was my age, maybe two years older than me. And we grew up together, practically. And we were holding each other. We were sharing whatever we had together, whatever-- with a bite of food, or anything we found, or had extra to organize something. Like a brother. Funfteichen and late December, he got swollen feet. He got water in his feet. Like, no, like this, heavy. And he couldn't walk. And he schlepped himself out to the [NON-ENGLISH] to work. And I helped him in the morning and at night. And he said, look, Sol, [NON-ENGLISH]. He called me by my Jewish name. I can't take it anymore. And I can't. I'm going to take my life. I'm going to do something drastic. And I start saying Yankev Hirsch, and I still believed somehow that my parents are still alive. I still didn't believe. I didn't want to think. And even we thought that they went to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they went and they-- they burned them. I couldn't believe it and I couldn't take it here. I couldn't. And I said, how am I going to come home? We've got neighbors at home. He was the [NON-ENGLISH] son, from the town. And then how I'm going to come home to your father and mother, and what I'm going to tell them? You took your life and it's against everything we stand for. You crazy [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: It was similar. And anyway, so we kept on going, day by day. And then a week before the evacuation, I-- on the [NON-ENGLISH] he worked on one end from the-- from the factory. And I worked on the other. And we're going home. And I don't-- I look to see my friend, Yankev Hirsch. He's not around. And all of a sudden, I see him over there and they put him in a wagon, in a stretcher, what they put to dead people also. He went ahead, plus he had the swollen feet. He fell to the oven. They had ovens, you know, burning. It was very cold outside. We worked outside from the factory. We-- [NON-ENGLISH] we made for-- for-- for different things, pipes, very deep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he burnt his whole hand. You know? Only they shall take him into the hospital. And that will tell you, to be at the hospital. It's a place what you don't have to go to work. You know? And they gave him the same food or something, and kept them in that barrack, and didn't have to go to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where I worked, I pushed the wagon, went behind. Took him into the camp. Took him over to there, to the rear. And I saw that they patched him up with a thing. And they kept him in there. And a week later, somehow, close to it, I found out that tomorrow morning the camp is evacuated and they're moving the camp. Everybody's leaving the camp. I ran over there and I said, Yankev Hirsch, please, get yourself together. Come over. Come onto the barrack. By tomorrow they evacuate. He goes, I don't care. I am not-- I can't walk. And I can't go. I'm-- I don't care.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, Yankev Hirsch, they're going to put gasoline. And remember the guys came home and they told stories? That they put gasoline and they burned the people? This is what they're going to do to you. Look, I said, how can I come home to my parents, your brother, sister, how could he do that to me? What do you-- what? I start crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, [NON-ENGLISH], I can't walk. And he couldn't. He couldn't walk. And he had the hand, burnt himself to get in. And I-- we cried together. We kissed each other. And I left him there. I had to leave. And early in the morning, they evacuated the whole camp. And we went from that camp, we marched.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what happened to people that couldn't move? They left them there? Or did they transport them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left them there. They left them there. And yeah. And we marched. In the morning it was bitter, bitter cold, bitter cold. And I had a blanket on me. And I pin, like this, pants, striped pants, and the jacket and the cap. A pin, and a blanket. I had a good blanket from there. I took it from the-- what, from the barrack. And we walked. I was young. And I-- I put myself on the-- on the thing and I told him, and then came to myself back, and strong. There in Funfteichen. And then you heard bangs. Sh-- bang, bang. And I saw anybody fell down, just suppose to shot them on the spot. In the snow, as we walked, you fell, bang. You had to use a-- a-- a bullet, the-- the Germans. The Gestapos or the soldiers, whatever you want to-- We marched the first night. We marched the day and the night, and another day. Until when we came to a farm one day, one night-- evening, and there we stopped. Now, the cold was un-- below, no, 10 below zero, whatever. If you walked, you could have heard a scribble on the ice. The snow was-- and anyway, we-- people fell-- are falling like fly-- flies. We came to that farm. And that farm had hay. And they took out hay. And they put up on the-- on the-- on the-- on the snow. And there-- there is where we slept. Right--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were-- we-- the camp must have been-- I would say, a couple of thousand-- 8,000-- 8,000 or 10,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They all marched?

speaker: interviewee
text: All marched.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they stopped at the one farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole camp marched. We arrived to the farm, the first farm over-- night, day and night, half of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Half. In the morning-- now, I was in the age. So I remember, like today, I worked-- maneuvered around. And I got myself in by the farm, in the hay, in the top. And I slept in the hay, in the-- in the inside, not the outside. Now, in the morning, bang. They shout, out, out, out, out, out. Early in the morning. Looked out. And I came out. Half of the population was frozen on the floor, on the snow. Half. And-- and we went further. I mean, forget about it. The people was less than half. And we got into Gross Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, again, did you pass through people's towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Towns. Yes, yes, yeah we passed the towns and the villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the people do when you were marching through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. They just walked away, walked away, made room. Went on into the houses. Just went into the houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they make fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, nothing. Nothing. Nothing, whatsoever. Just went into the houses. And some of them looked. Nothing, whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they offer no water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't offer.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, nothing. Nothing, nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which country was this now, Poland or Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Germany. In Germany, from Funfteichen to Gross Rosen. I arrived in Gross Rosen. I was already knocked out, weak, weak, weak, weak.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, when you arrived, was there any kind of registration? Where you had-- did you have to register? Did they clean you or shower you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or delouse you with something?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no No No more. no more. No. No. At Gross Rosen, they had barracks. They wasn't so anymore-- I think it was in the-- in a city. Like a city, floors. We walked up steps. There was floors of people. Floors, in the city, Gross Rosen. And they did have-- I understand they had had I think, a crematorium in there. Where it was, what location it was, I don't know. I don't know. And-- and they made a selection soon as we arrived that evening. Through two evenings that I was there in that-- upstairs. And in the building they had floors, and big steps, what we walked. One floor, two floors, three floors, four floors, people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean it was big-- it wasn't a regular barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was not barracks like it was in the camps up to that place. And they made a selection on the appellplatz. They selected me. Why they selected me, I didn't know. And I said, this is it. I'm finished. And they took us up on the third floor. And as I walked up on the third floor, they put up that thing-- a-- a cross on my chest, and put me in, in a section. And they had an iron gate, like a prison, and put me in there. On the floor there was some hay. And there was a lot of other [NON-ENGLISH], other people like me, laying on the floor. And one by one, the whole line, enough to go to sleep. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just were lying on the floor? No-- no boards, no beds of any kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Nothing, nothing. Just floor and a little bit of hay. And I-- I was knocked-out tired. I finally went to sleep. [THUNDER] And that was the second night, I think, what I arrived to that camp. The first night I don't remember. The second night, I think, Gross Rosen. And in the middle of the night, around-- we didn't know what time it was. And the-- the guy came to the gate and knocked. And he says, [YIDDISH]. He called me [YIDDISH.] Wake up, my brother. He was sleeping near me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I turn around. And then I look. Uh-huh, give him a-- he's dead. Now, [NON-ENGLISH]. He is dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he starts crying at the gate. Polish. He comes from Lodz, or Russia, I don't know from where. And then crying. With them, together three years. And we were together. And I'm holding him, staying there. [THUNDER] And now I could have saved him through the-- this. You know, they're leaving, a transport was leaving for work tomorrow morning. For this Funfteichen camp. People, where they send to the gas chambers, burning them all the time, Funfteichen camp. Help me. You know, it went into my head, of help me. And I looked, OK, [YIDDISH]. He was older. He called me [YIDDISH]. Then I take the-- takes the spit. And he tries to wash off the crayon-- the cross. And I-- and we work hard. And we close up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we talk of the whole thing. And somehow he worked-- it wasn't too hard to open up that gate. You know? The-- and he opened the gate, and I got out. Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Took me out. And I don't know, still to this day, who the man was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you escaped from the-- from the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No. No, no, no, no, no, no. I came out. He took me out for to escape from taking me to the gas chamber, to the death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: With this, you know, he took me out. He saved my life. And he saved me. In the morning, early in the morning, they-- we were put in a train again. Counted. They gave us coffee. Only coffee. And we went to Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now, you said they counted you. They didn't have names? Like, if somebody was missing, they wouldn't know who it was? When you-- when you came in they didn't take--

speaker: interviewee
text: They only count on the appellplatz. When we left the place, they counted. When we arrived, they counted.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said by the appellplatz, isn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the appellplatz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't they call names there?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? No names. No names. No names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No names. They didn't need them anymore. They didn't even use the numbers. Only-- only--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just counting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Counting. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were never given any number on your arm anywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Only a number here. Well, it was only in Auschwitz they gave the numbers. And in Auschwitz, I was only three days. From Gross Rosen we went by train to Mauthausen. Mauthausen, we stayed there only, also, two or three nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only two or three nights. Two, most. I remember it like today. And I was sent over to-- well, they took us over by train again. Harzug. Harzug is the [INAUDIBLE] factories. Again, the [INAUDIBLE] factory is Harzug. Harzug was a labor camp. Also, the same going out to work in the morning, and come back at night. And also, a terrible time. They brought in people, dead people in the bathroom, and all over dead people there, wherever you went. And wasn't there for long. There was a very late February, or April. No, February, we were there. I was in Harzug about a couple of weeks. Worked at a [NON-ENGLISH]. Had an incident there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They-- they made selections every so often for the-- for the crematorium, for the Muselmanner. Instead they should fall on them for work, they took them just away, they shouldn't be selected. They selected me. And I know why they did. I know there are things I went through with. And I knew all the-- the-- the-- when and what. I mean, you'll-- you'll get that hungry, you learn from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Experience, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Experience. And I saw the Muselmanner, and I was one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you got so skinny again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. And they selected me. And I was smaller. I was smaller, but I had-- I grew here, in the United States. Small and skinny, and whatnot. And I had a broken teeth. They never-- OK, three teeth, and my teeth were knocked out. OK. I selected. And they were counting for the going out to work. And as they select us there, about 20, 30 people, what were already they selected to go-- well to-- for their death, I start screaming. I saw the bathroom. Just screaming, like a wild Indian, I would say today, screaming. [NON-ENGLISH]. I had a bellyache. Ooh, ooh. So we couldn't-- you know, the SS man was there, the kapo. Go. I should go into the bathroom. Bathroom, I went in. There was a barrack. And there were dead people. And they had my number taken already, 3642. Write that number down. There it is I changed the number. And I went into the bathroom. There was a window. Jumped out through the window. Nobody there. It's in the camp. And went over to one of the dead people, took off the my jacket, and just throw it down there, underneath and took his jacket, the number, and cleaned myself up and just mixed in to the-- with the-- with the people. And right away, I somehow worked myself in and went to work that morning. Another way I stayed alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't quite understand. Do you mean-- what difference did it make which jacket you had on? Because your number was off already?

speaker: interviewee
text: The number was written down. 3642.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The 30 people what they selected for the-- for the crematorium or for the death-- death, they put on the numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I know all this, you know, was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew the tricks, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's-- then I saved my life and I went into work. And then it didn't take long from there. This was, already, late. Late, late, late. in April, April, 19--5.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's now been a year since you were taken into camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. So yeah, but yeah. And from-- from there, they put us in the wagons, again. They evacuated the camp. And we were a couple of days, day and night, no food. And we arrived to--

speaker: interviewer
text: No water?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bergen-Belsen. Bergen-Belsen was-- if any of the camps were bad, this was the worst. People on the streets, hundreds of people, dead. Laying, not taken care of. Typhus, sicknesses, what you want. Lice is crawling. Rats, what you want to have. What-- see, OK, sicknesses anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, we're coming to the end of the tape now. We'll talk more about Bergen-Belsen on the new tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
